does: SLYP is an agentic vulnerability discovery pipeline that identifies race condition vulnerabilities in Windows COM binaries and automatically generates verified proof-of-concept exploits. The system exposes three categories of reusable tool interfaces—binary exploration, COM inspection, and dynamic debugging—that agents can leverage to reason about COM objects' behavior and timing-sensitive races. Evaluated on a benchmark of 20 COM objects containing 40 vulnerability cases, SLYP achieves 0.973 F1 for vulnerability discovery, substantially outperforming production coding agents by 0.208 F1 and state-of-the-art static analyzers by 3.3x. The pipeline moves from vulnerability identification through to debugger-verified PoC generation, with production agents failing to verify exploits when these specialized tools are unavailable.
connects: This work extends prior research on automated vulnerability discovery and agentic reasoning in security analysis. It builds on recent advances in large language models applied to code understanding and exploit generation, while responding to limitations of static analysis approaches that struggle with race conditions requiring temporal reasoning. The paper connects to work on binary analysis tooling and symbolic execution, adapting these concepts for agentic exploration. It also draws from research on tool use in agents and interactive debugging assistance, combining these threads to address the specific challenge of COM binary vulnerabilities—a surface area that existing approaches underutilize despite the security-critical nature of privilege-escalation attacks targeting Windows services.
enables: This work points toward broader applications of agentic vulnerability reasoning on other privileged Windows services and system components beyond COM. The modular tool interface architecture suggests future extensions to different binary formats, operating systems, and vulnerability classes requiring temporal or interactive reasoning. The demonstrated effectiveness of combining static analysis with dynamic feedback through debugger integration enables follow-up work on automated exploit generation for race conditions in other domains. The benchmark itself establishes a foundation for measuring progress on COM-specific security tasks. More broadly, SLYP's approach to grounding agentic reasoning with specialized domain tools and verification feedback could inform safer, more reliable automated security analysis pipelines for critical systems.
